stem: The student wants to explain what makes hummingbirds' flight ability unique. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hummingbirds can fly backward because their shoulder joint structure allows their wings to rotate in a full circle, unlike most other birds whose wings can only move up and down.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most birds can only move their wings up and down, but hummingbirds have a unique shoulder joint structure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backward, and most other birds can only move their wings up and down.

choices.label: D
choices.text: With wings that can rotate in a full circle, hummingbirds have a unique shoulder joint structure.

explanation: Choice A most effectively accomplishes the goal because it clearly establishes the causal relationship between the hummingbirds' anatomical feature (shoulder joint structure that allows full wing rotation) and their unique flight ability (flying backward). It also provides important context by contrasting this with other birds' limitations, making the uniqueness clear. This choice synthesizes all relevant information into a coherent explanation of cause and effect.

distractorRationale.A: This is the correct answer as it provides the complete causal explanation.
distractorRationale.B: This choice mentions the unique shoulder joint but fails to connect it to the backward flight ability, leaving the explanation incomplete. It doesn't explain what makes hummingbirds' flight unique—it only states they have a different shoulder structure without explaining the functional consequence.
distractorRationale.C: This choice states the unique ability (flying backward) and mentions other birds' limitations but omits the anatomical explanation entirely. Without mentioning the shoulder joint or wing rotation, it fails to explain what makes this ability possible.
distractorRationale.D: This choice mentions the wing rotation and shoulder joint but never connects these features to the backward flight ability. It doesn't accomplish the goal of explaining what makes their flight unique because it never mentions the actual flight capability that distinguishes hummingbirds.

passage:
While researching a report on local wildlife, a student took the following notes:

• Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backward.
• Hummingbirds have a unique shoulder joint structure.
• This shoulder joint allows their wings to rotate in a full circle.
• Most other birds can only move their wings up and down.
